problems.problem: Hiring a Sales Closer for B2B enterprise deals
problems.description: In 'nov 2024 my first million', Tony explicitly states he needs to hire a Sales Closer to handle the main talking/relationship-building parts of B2B sales calls while he focuses on product questions. He's closing big deals (3,000-seat, 1,000-seat contracts) but recognizes he's terrible at the relationship-building, follow-up, and persuasion aspects of sales, which drains his energy. He mentions good incoming requests but needs someone with skills to handle them properly.
evidence: Going forward, I'd prefer to get a salesperson to do the talking part for me. I think a 'Sales Closer' position is what it's called.
Just need someone with the skills to handle it. I'll still be on the calls as much as I can to answer information/product-related questions, but I'll leave the main talking part to the salesperson.
Partnering with an experienced sales team is also an option I'm exploring. Hopefully, I can figure it out.
Other than the Sales Closer role that I'm hiring, I've also started working with a design agency
problems.email_open_reasoning: Tony explicitly states he's hiring for this role and describes it as a clear need. He's already closing big enterprise deals but recognizes this skill gap is limiting growth. A stranger with relevant sales experience or connections would get immediate attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Hiring is typically a 2-4 month process, and he's actively pursuing this now. Given the B2B focus is central to his growth strategy and he has incoming demand, this need will persist until filled and the person is onboarded.

problems.problem: Support needed for Product Hunt launch tomorrow
problems.description: In the post "early bird license of my new product," Tony explicitly asks for support for his TypingMind.com Product Hunt launch happening the next day. He emphasizes this is his third attempt at building AI products (the last two didn't go well) and directly requests his audience to join and support the launch.
evidence: And please join me on my Product Hunt launch tomorrow. I really appreciate any support! 😊
As you may have known, this is my 3rd attempt at building products with AI. The last two didn't go so well, and I'm so glad that it has finally worked!
TypingMind.com is launching tomorrow on Product Hunt!
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is an explicit ask for help with a time-sensitive launch happening the next day. The author directly states he needs support and would appreciate it. The stakes are clear (third attempt after two failures), and the timing is urgent (tomorrow). A stranger offering specific Product Hunt launch assistance would get immediate attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a one-day event. By the time this is analyzed (March 10, 2023, with launch the next day), the Product Hunt launch has likely already occurred and this specific need is resolved.
problems.source_post: early bird license of my new product

problems.problem: Beta testers needed for Midday email client launching December
problems.description: In 'I'm building a new email client,' Tony announces he's releasing a public beta of Midday (his new email client) in December. He explicitly asks people to sign up for the waitlist to try the beta version. The app doesn't have all features yet and he needs users to test it alongside their existing email clients.
evidence: I plan to release the first public beta sometime in December.
It doesn't have all the features yet, so if you can't replace all of your email usage with Midday. For example, I'm currently using both Midday and my previous email client in parallel.
If you are interested and want to give it a try, sign up for the waitlist here, and I'll send out the beta version soon!
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is an explicit ask for beta testers ahead of a December launch. Tony has been working on this for years and is now actively recruiting users. A stranger offering relevant beta testing help, feedback, or connections to potential users would likely get a response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The beta launch is planned for December (less than a month away), and the initial beta testing phase will likely run 1-2 months. After that, the need shifts from beta testers to general users/customers.
problems.source_post: im building a new email client

problems.problem: Need beta readers & feedback for indie hacking book before July launch
problems.description: In 'Jun 2025 update: Finishing my book', Tony is finishing his indie hacking book with an official July 2025 release deadline. He's sent early drafts to friends and made multiple rewrites, and is now opening up preview access to gather more feedback before the official launch. He explicitly invites readers to pre-order and opt in to read the preview version.
evidence: After pre-order, you will receive a survey link with some questions that will help me shape the book to be more useful. You can also opt in to read the preview version!
For the past months, I've sent out the early draft of the book to some friends for feedback (thanks to them, I rewrote and restructured the book at least 2 times)
It's now at a state where I'm more comfortable letting more people read the preview version and share feedback.
If you opt in to read the draft, I can't wait to hear your feedback about the content before officially releasing it.
problems.email_open_reasoning: Tony explicitly asks for beta readers and feedback multiple times in the post, mentions he's already incorporated feedback that led to 2 complete rewrites, and has a hard deadline (July 2025). This is a clear, urgent collaboration need from someone actively seeking outside input.
problems.persistence_reasoning: The official book release is scheduled for July 2025, which is next month from the post date (June 2025). Once the book launches, the need for pre-launch feedback will be resolved.

problems.problem: Finding the right ad targeting to reach pro LLM users for Typing Mind
problems.description: In 'april 2024 updates new product', Tony describes a failed LinkedIn Ads experiment that cost $2,000 with $0 revenue. He identifies specific targeting challenges: he can't reach his ideal audience (business owners on Desktop macOS/iOS, pro users experienced with AI agents/LLM) through LinkedIn's targeting options and is uncertain about the right advertising approach.
evidence: I spent ~$2,000 running a LinkedIn Ads experiment. The result was not good, $0 revenue.

It's difficult to target my desired audience using LinkedIn Ads. For example, if I want to show ads only to business owners who are using a Desktop on macOS or iOS, which is the highest converting audience, I can't.
My target audience is pro users who are experienced in building AI agents or working with LLM. Maybe I'll need to change the way I approach this audience
I'll continue to try Ads again next time with Facebook Ads.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is clearly an active, costly problem. Tony just spent $2,000 learning what doesn't work and explicitly states he's planning to try Facebook Ads next. He's actively seeking solutions for customer acquisition, making this a real business blocker. A stranger with relevant targeting expertise would likely get a response.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Customer acquisition is an ongoing challenge for any SaaS. Tony is actively experimenting with different channels (LinkedIn Ads failed, YouTube channel starting, Facebook Ads planned next), suggesting this will remain a priority for at least the next 6 months as he tests and iterates.

problems.problem: Scaling Typing Mind with a team vs. staying solo
problems.description: In "another 6 figure exit and the future," Tony faces growing pressure to expand Typing Mind beyond his solo operation. He's working only 4 hours/day with freelancers, but feels Typing Mind needs "a whole team to push this forward and capture a larger chunk of the growing market" based on growing customer demand and inbound leads. He's torn between not wanting to hire/manage a team, not wanting to work more hours, potentially selling if a good offer comes, or continuing as-is.
evidence: One thing that stresses me out a bit is the pressure to get more people on the team. I feel like Typing Mind needs a lot more attention than I'm giving it right now, it needs a whole team to push this forward and capture a larger chunk of the growing market.
Me working just 4 hours a day with some freelancers is probably not going to cut it. But I don't want to work more, I also don't want to hire and manage a team.
Maybe I'll have to do it eventually, or maybe I will sell the whole thing if someone has a good offer.
This is judging from the growing demand from customers and inbound leads I got over the past few months.
problems.email_open_reasoning: Tony explicitly states this is stressing him out and identifies it as a current strategic dilemma with real consequences (missing market opportunity). He's actively wrestling with three incompatible desires: not working more, not managing a team, but capturing growth. A stranger offering a concrete solution (fractional team model, growth partner, etc.) would likely get attention.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Typing Mind is generating $40K+/month and the AI market is rapidly growing, so this tension will intensify over the next 6+ months. The problem won't resolve quickly unless he makes a decisive choice to hire, sell, or accept the tradeoff.
problems.source_post: another 6 figure exit and the future

problems.problem: Need marketing/SEO help for TypingMind beyond organic traffic
problems.description: In '500k milestone my reflections after 1 year of building typing mind', Tony describes spending all his time on product development with 'very little time to do anything else like marketing, SEO, trying paid ads, cold outreach, building community, etc.' He states he can't rely solely on organic traffic forever and is considering hiring someone to do 'real marketing,' but this comes with 'a different set of problems I need to learn to solve.'
evidence: I had to spend all of my time building and improving the product. Very little time to do anything else like marketing, SEO, trying paid ads, cold outreach, building community, etc.
However, to create a sustainable business, I'm not sure I can rely solely on organic traffic forever.
I've been considering doing some 'real marketing' or at least hiring someone to do it, but this comes with a different set of problems I need to learn to solve.
problems.email_open_reasoning: This is explicitly stated as a real concern for business sustainability. Tony recognizes organic traffic won't last forever and has explicitly considered hiring help, indicating he'd likely engage with relevant expertise. However, he frames it as something he's 'been considering' rather than actively seeking, preventing it from scoring higher.
problems.persistence_reasoning: As TypingMind continues growing and the AI space becomes more competitive, this marketing gap will become increasingly urgent. Tony is already aware of the issue and considering solutions, suggesting it will remain a priority for at least 6 months until he either hires someone or develops a marketing strategy.
problems.source_post: 500k milestone my reflections after 1 year of building typing mind

problems.problem: Need to learn B2B sales & demo calls for Typing Mind Custom
problems.description: In "2023 recap," Tony explicitly discusses wanting to be "more aggressive with marketing" for Typing Mind Custom and considering hiring salespeople or doing it himself first. He acknowledges he's never done B2B sales before and identifies specific skills he needs: generating leads, cold outreach, doing demo calls, following up, and closing deals.
evidence: I'm thinking about being more aggressive with the marketing and could reach out for Typing Mind Custom. Like hiring salespeople and letting them get more business customers somehow.
Maybe I'll do it myself first. There are some good learning opportunities in the process: generating leads, cold outreach, doing demo calls, following up, and closing the deal.
I've never done this myself, so part of me wants to do it.
The only thing is that 'doing a demo call' conflicts with my current 'no meeting policy.' And I heard only 10% of the demo calls end up being a successful deal, so that's discouraging.
problems.email_open_reasoning: Tony explicitly states he's considering doing this and has never done it before. He identifies it as a learning opportunity and is actively weighing whether to hire salespeople or do it himself. The conflict with his 'no meeting policy' and discouragement about demo call conversion rates suggests he's genuinely stuck on how to proceed. This is clearly actionable and related to his primary business focus.
problems.persistence_reasoning: Typing Mind Custom is his primary focus and he's explicitly considering becoming more aggressive with B2B sales. This is a strategic capability he needs to build for business growth, not a short-term tactical issue. However, he may hire someone or decide against it within 6 months.

problems.problem: Making Typing Mind Custom self-served without 'Contact Us' calls
problems.description: In 'july 2023 6k mrr getting back on recurring revenue,' Tony is transitioning into B2B sales for Typing Mind Custom but strongly dislikes the 'Contact Us' model requiring emails and calls. He wants to create generic packages that can be sold autonomously without manual sales processes, but needs to understand B2B customer needs first to build the right packages.
evidence: I've always hated the 'Contact Us' button. I wanted everything to be self-served, which is stress-free for me.
So I'll have to adapt to doing business with B2B customers, a lot of emails & calls. A lot of things to learn.

My plan is to get in touch with these businesses, understand what they need, and how to create some good generic packages that can fit most of them, then I'll sell those packages fully autonomously without the 'Contact Us' button.
Really hope this works.
problems.email_open_reasoning: Tony explicitly states his strong desire to eliminate the 'Contact Us' model and achieve self-service B2B sales. He's actively working on this problem ('Really hope this works' shows uncertainty) and it's a clear business priority affecting his stress levels and work model. Solutions that help automate B2B sales or create productized packages would be highly relevant.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a structural challenge tied to scaling Typing Mind Custom's B2B revenue. He's just starting to onboard B2B customers and needs several months to understand patterns, build packages, and transition to self-service. This is a medium-term business model transformation.
problems.source_post: july 2023 6k mrr getting back on recurring revenue

problems.problem: Building a minimal team to explore other product ideas beyond TypingMind
problems.description: In 'nov 2024 my first million', Tony states that TypingMind has consumed all his time and he has other product ideas (like an email app) he wants to pursue but can't justify the opportunity cost. He's trying to set up a minimal team specifically to explore these other ideas, though he admits he 'really doesn't like managing people.'
evidence: I have ideas and plans to build other products, like the email app that I have always talked about. But every time I spend my time on something else, I feel like I can gain much more value by using that time for TypingMind…
In the near future, I will try to set up a minimal team to help me explore my other ideas. I really don't like managing people, but I guess this is the only way.
I'm also slowly expanding my team to have more capacity to work on TypingMind.
problems.email_open_reasoning: While Tony mentions this plan, it's less urgent than the Sales Closer role. He's ambivalent about it ('I really don't like managing people') and TypingMind clearly takes priority. He'd read carefully but may not be ready to act immediately on a stranger's outreach.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a long-term structural challenge as TypingMind continues to dominate his attention. However, his ambivalence about management and the fact that it's for exploratory projects (not core business) suggests he may delay or deprioritize this indefinitely.

problems.problem: Preparing Typing Mind Custom for larger marketing push
problems.description: In 'july 2023 6k mrr getting back on recurring revenue,' Tony mentions he hasn't put much effort into marketing Typing Mind Custom yet because he feels 'it is not quite ready.' He's currently only advertising in the footer of Typing Mind, but with $6K MRR and growing B2B interest, he needs to determine when and how to scale marketing efforts.
evidence: I haven't put much effort into marketing for this one yet, as I feel it is not quite ready.
The only place I'm advertising this is in the footer of Typing Mind
I'm looking forward to onboarding more customers to the Custom version soon.
problems.email_open_reasoning: While Tony mentions this constraint, it's less urgent than his self-service challenge. The product is already generating $6K MRR with minimal marketing, so there's no immediate crisis. He's positioning this as a future opportunity rather than a current blocker requiring external help.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This is a near-term tactical decision that will likely resolve within a few months as Tony completes self-hosting capabilities and onboards more B2B customers. Once the product feels 'ready,' he'll likely begin marketing efforts.
problems.source_post: july 2023 6k mrr getting back on recurring revenue

problems.problem: Finding effective marketing channels beyond word-of-mouth for TypingMind
problems.description: In 'oct 2025 updates code money and travel,' Tony explicitly states he's struggling with marketing: 'Most of my sales are from word-of-mouth. I've been attempting other marketing channels from time to time, but so far, nothing has worked for me.' He spent $600 on a paid ad campaign that resulted in only 2 conversions. While he says he's 'not worried too much about it,' he's actively experimenting with different channels, suggesting this is an ongoing challenge.
evidence: Most of my sales are from word-of-mouth. I've been attempting other marketing channels from time to time, but so far, nothing has worked for me.
In my latest paid ad campaign, I've spent $600 so far, resulting in only 2 conversions.
I really suck at marketing in general, but I'm not worried too much about it.
problems.email_open_reasoning: While Tony acknowledges marketing is a weakness and is experimenting with it, he explicitly states he's 'not worried too much about it' and believes customers will come naturally with a good product. The business is already making $130-160k/month from word-of-mouth, so this isn't an urgent blocker. A cold email about marketing strategies might be read with mild interest but likely wouldn't prompt immediate engagement from a stranger.
problems.persistence_reasoning: This appears to be an ongoing operational challenge for TypingMind. While not urgent now due to strong organic growth, as the product matures and word-of-mouth slows, marketing will become more important. Tony is experimenting intermittently, suggesting this will remain a background concern for at least 6 months.
problems.source_post: oct 2025 updates code money and travel

problems.problem: Launching image.social beta - finishing 'small but important tasks'
problems.description: In 'april 2024 updates new product', Tony announces image.social is in beta but needs to complete 'some small but important tasks' before launch. He plans to finish these 'in the next weeks when I have the free time', suggesting time constraints around finalizing this product.
evidence: As of now, the product is still in beta, I plan to finish some small but important tasks for it and launch in the next weeks when I have the free time.
I plan to make this a micro SaaS that will run on its own.
I have had the idea for a long time, it came from the pain of setting up open-graph images for every page when creating new products.
problems.email_open_reasoning: While this is a real product in development, the language is vague ('small but important tasks', 'when I have the free time') and suggests this isn't currently urgent. He doesn't specify what help he needs or indicate being blocked. It's more of a side project he'll get to when time allows.
problems.persistence_reasoning: He plans to launch 'in the next weeks', suggesting this is a short-term task. Once launched as a 'micro SaaS that will run on its own', the problem resolves. The vagueness also suggests he may deprioritize it if Typing Mind demands more attention.
